front: Define the gradient of a scalar function f(x, y, z).
back: The gradient of a scalar function f is a vector field denoted by ∇f or grad f. It points in the direction of the greatest rate of increase of the function. In Cartesian coordinates, it is defined as: ∇f = (∂f/∂x)i + (∂f/∂y)j + (∂f/∂z)k.

A partial derivative of a function of multiple variables is its derivative with respect to one of those variables, while holding the other variables constant. It measures the instantaneous rate of change of the function in that specific direction.

front: Define the directional derivative D_u f at a point.
back: The directional derivative measures the rate at which the function f changes at a point in the direction of a unit vector u. It is calculated as the dot product of the gradient of f and the unit vector u: D_u f = ∇f • u.

The Jacobian matrix is a matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function. For a function F: Rⁿ → Rᵐ, the Jacobian J is an m×n matrix where the element J_ij is the partial derivative of the i-th component function with respect to the j-th variable: J_ij = ∂F_i/∂x_j.

front: State the chain rule for partial derivatives for a function z = f(x(t), y(t)).
back: The chain rule for this case states that the total derivative of z with respect to t is: dz/dt = (∂f/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂f/∂y)(dy/dt). It accounts for how z changes with t through both intermediate variables x and y.
